initial_claims.description: An attacker can grow the storage array to a large size, after which `clearDOS()` attempts to zero every storage slot by reassigning the array to a new empty array. In Solidity 0.4.x this storage clear cost scales with array length and can exceed the block gas limit, permanently preventing successful execution of `clearDOS()`.
initial_claims.evidence: insertNnumbers(uint value, uint numbers) lets any caller append an arbitrary number of elements by increasing `array.length` inside a loop and writing `array[numElements++] = value`. Later, clearDOS() executes `array = new uint[](0);` after only requiring `numElements > 1500`. Because the array length is attacker-controlled and unbounded, an attacker can first call insertNnumbers() repeatedly to make `array.length` very large, then any call to clearDOS() will require gas proportional to the full storage array size and eventually revert out-of-gas. Exploit path: (1) attacker repeatedly calls insertNnumbers(..., largeN) to bloat storage, (2) once `numElements > 1500`, calls to clearDOS() become too expensive to fit in a block, (3) the clearing function is effectively DoS'd.

claim.description: An attacker can grow the storage array to a large size, after which `clearDOS()` attempts to zero every storage slot by reassigning the array to a new empty array. In Solidity 0.4.x this storage clear cost scales with array length and can exceed the block gas limit, permanently preventing successful execution of `clearDOS()`.
claim.evidence: insertNnumbers(uint value, uint numbers) lets any caller append an arbitrary number of elements by increasing `array.length` inside a loop and writing `array[numElements++] = value`. Later, clearDOS() executes `array = new uint[](0);` after only requiring `numElements > 1500`. Because the array length is attacker-controlled and unbounded, an attacker can first call insertNnumbers() repeatedly to make `array.length` very large, then any call to clearDOS() will require gas proportional to the full storage array size and eventually revert out-of-gas. Exploit path: (1) attacker repeatedly calls insertNnumbers(..., largeN) to bloat storage, (2) once `numElements > 1500`, calls to clearDOS() become too expensive to fit in a block, (3) the clearing function is effectively DoS'd.

verdict.reasoning: The pattern exists in the code. `insertNnumbers(uint value,uint numbers)` is public and unbounded; inside its loop it grows the storage array with `array.length += 1;` and writes `array[numElements++] = value;`, so any external caller can make `array.length` arbitrarily large over multiple transactions. In `clearDOS()`, after only `require(numElements>1500);`, the statement `array = new uint[](0);` resets a storage dynamic array. In Solidity 0.4.x, clearing a storage array requires touching its existing storage slots, so gas cost scales with the prior array length. Because the length is attacker-controlled and unbounded, there is a realistic point where `clearDOS()` cannot fit within the block gas limit and will always run out of gas. There is no code-backed mitigation: no access control, no cap on `numbers`, no cap on total length, and no incremental/bounded deletion logic in `clearDOS()`. The defender's argument is consistent with the code. The exploit path is physically achievable on mainnet by repeatedly calling `insertNnumbers` to bloat storage, then causing `clearDOS()` to become uncallable. Impact is limited to denial of that cleanup function; the contract still has `clear()` which sets `numElements = 0` without clearing storage, so this is not a complete contract DoS and severity is lower than claimed.
verdict.recommendation: Do not clear an unbounded storage array in one transaction. Replace `clearDOS()` with a bounded cleanup pattern, e.g. delete a fixed number of elements per call and track progress, or avoid physical deletion entirely by using only `numElements = 0` if logical clearing is sufficient. Also add limits on `numbers` / maximum array size or restrict who can grow the array.

In `clearDOS()`, after only `require(numElements>1500);`, the statement `array = new uint[](0);` resets a storage dynamic array. In Solidity 0.4.x, clearing a storage array requires touching its existing storage slots, so gas cost scales with the prior array length. Because the length is attacker-controlled and unbounded, there is a realistic point where `clearDOS()` cannot fit within the block gas limit and will always run out of gas. There is no code-backed mitigation: no access control, no cap on `numbers`, no cap on total length, and no incremental/bounded deletion logic in `clearDOS()`. The defender's argument is consistent with the code. The exploit path is physically achievable on mainnet by repeatedly calling `insertNnumbers` to bloat storage, then causing `clearDOS()` to become uncallable. Impact is limited to denial of that cleanup function; the contract still has `clear()` which sets `numElements = 0` without clearing storage, so this is not a complete contract DoS and severity is lower than claimed.

The cited pattern is present exactly at `clearDOS()`. The function performs `array = new uint[](0);` on a storage dynamic array whose length is attacker-controlled and unbounded via the public `insertNnumbers(uint value,uint numbers)` loop. There is no access control, no cap on `numbers`, no cap on `array.length`, and no alternative bounded clear path inside `clearDOS()`. Therefore nothing in the code blocks the attack path where an attacker bloats `array` and makes `clearDOS()` exceed block gas limits.

Because the length is attacker-controlled and unbounded, there is a realistic point where `clearDOS()` cannot fit within the block gas limit and will always run out of gas.
